label: About
title: How this page is made
short_blurb: This page is generated from saved content signals in Obsidian, then turned into a daily scene, mapped into clickable artifacts, browser-verified, and archived as one edition in an ongoing series.
body: Content signals are collected in Obsidian first. That includes things like Twitter bookmarks, YouTube likes, clips, notes, outbound links, and recurring motifs that keep showing up across the vault. The vault is structured as an LLM Wiki, so those signals are not just stored as a pile of bookmarks. They are archived, linked, timestamped, and turned into a usable research field that can be mined for objects, materials, moods, media evidence, and unfinished fascinations.

From there, the site runs a daily pipeline. Recent Obsidian signals are mined, clustered into possible worlds, and expanded with automated research. That research is used for design direction only. It shapes the atmosphere, material language, object inventory, and scene logic, but the public source windows are still bound back to saved material from the vault rather than random filler from the open web. A new scene is then generated for the day, visible artifacts inside the image are mapped as interaction targets, source windows are attached to those objects, and the result is reviewed in the browser before it becomes the live edition and is archived.

The current generation works exactly this way. The live edition, 2026-04-23-forest-breath-cabinet-v2, came out of an Obsidian signal cluster around forest breath, Japanese environmental ambient, and ecological listening. It was generated as a humid cabinet-like world with eight mapped artifacts, including the Environmental Sound Catalogue, Central Habitat Vitrine, Watershed Map Board, and Open Leaf Study Book. Each of those objects is bound to a real saved YouTube source from the vault, including Takashi Kokubo's Blessings Of The Forest, Oasis Of The Wind / Forest Of Ion, Get at the Wave, and related ambient works. Browser review then verifies that the eight hotspots render correctly, open the right media, and behave like part of the scene rather than generic cards.

So the page is not a hand-composed composition. It is the current visible state of a system that collects signals, organizes them in Obsidian, turns them into a research field, generates a world from that field, binds real saved media back into the image, tests the result in a browser, and archives each daily edition as part of an ongoing series.
